edges.target: Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress and UPR Activation

edges.source: Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress and UPR Activation
Aberrant Type I Collagen Expression in Cartilage

edges.description: Failed endochondral growth of long bones produces severe limb shortening.
Failed vertebral body ossification manifests as flattened vertebrae and platyspondylic dwarfism.
Abnormal rib cartilage growth and ossification produce short ribs.
Defective pelvic cartilage ossification produces hypoplastic ilia.
Defective pelvic endochondral ossification delays pubic bone ossification.
Failed secondary ossification center development produces absent or delayed epiphyseal nuclei.
Abnormal growth plate organization and modeling produce metaphyseal flaring of long bones.
Defective vertebral endochondral ossification leaves many vertebral bodies unossified or minimally ossified.
edges.target: Absent or Minimally Ossified Vertebral Bodies

edges.description: Craniofacial cartilage and skeletal development are affected in the severe type II collagenopathy spectrum, producing micrognathia in some fetuses.
Severe disproportionate skeletal growth failure can make the cranium appear voluminous relative to the shortened trunk and limbs.
Short ribs and axial skeletal dysplasia produce a hypoplastic thorax, which restricts fetal lung development.
intermediate_mechanisms: Short ribs and hypoplastic thorax constrain fetal lung growth.

edges.description: Widespread skeletal ossification failure and thoracic restriction produce the lethal perinatal skeletal dysplasia phenotype.
intermediate_mechanisms: Severe thoracic restriction and respiratory compromise cause perinatal lethality.

edges.description: Thoracic hypoplasia produces the narrow chest phenotype.
Severe thoracic hypoplasia constrains fetal lung development and produces pulmonary hypoplasia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Restricted fetal thoracic cage limits lung expansion.

edges.description: Thoracic restriction and pulmonary underdevelopment produce progressive neonatal respiratory distress.
intermediate_mechanisms: Thoracic restriction and pulmonary hypoplasia reduce neonatal respiratory reserve.

edges.description: Severe fetal skeletal dysplasia and thoracic compromise can be accompanied by hydropic appearance, but the immediate path between COL2A1 skeletal disease and hydrops is unresolved.
Severe fetal skeletal dysplasia can be associated with polyhydramnios, but the immediate causal pathway is not established in the available evidence.

nodes.description: In hypochondrogenesis, chondrocytes aberrantly express type I collagen genes (COL1A1, COL1A2) alongside COL2A1, which is normally absent from hyaline cartilage. This pathological reprogramming of gene expression likely reflects cellular stress responses and further disrupts normal cartilage ECM composition.
nodes.id: Aberrant Type I Collagen Expression in Cartilage

evidence.reference_title: Mutation in the COL2A1 gene in a patient with hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: In situ hybridization with cRNA probes revealed a significant level of alpha 1(I) mRNA in the cytoplasm of the patient's chondrocytes.

nodes.description: Vertebral body ossification is absent or severely reduced in hypochondrogenesis, affecting many vertebral levels and producing a characteristic radiographic pattern.
nodes.id: Absent or Minimally Ossified Vertebral Bodies

evidence.reference_title: Type II achondrogenesis-hypochondrogenesis: morphologic and immunohistopathologic studies.
evidence.snippet: absence of ossification of sacrum, pubis, ischia, tali, calcanei, and many vertebral bodies; the long bones were short with mild metaphyseal flaring

evidence.reference_title: Hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: The delay in vertebral ossification, the absence of all the epiphyseal nuclei and of the tarsal bones might suggest the diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis

nodes.description: Chronic unresolved ER stress transitions from a protective response to pro-apoptotic signaling, leading to premature chondrocyte death in the growth plate. This reduces the population of proliferating chondrocytes available for normal cartilage and bone formation.

evidence.reference_title: Modeling type II collagenopathy skeletal dysplasia by directed conversion and induced pluripotent stem cells.
evidence.snippet: The COL2pathy-iChon cells showed suppressed expression of COL2A1 and significant apoptosis. A distended endoplasmic reticulum (ER) was detected, thus suggesting the adaptation of gene expression and cell death caused by excess ER stress.

nodes.description: Absence of all epiphyseal nuclei and delayed ossification of tarsal bones and other secondary ossification centers.

evidence.reference_title: Hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: The delay in vertebral ossification, the absence of all the epiphyseal nuclei and of the tarsal bones might suggest the diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis

nodes.description: Ossification of the pubic bones is delayed prenatally.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis using fetal MRI: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Fetal MR findings were the presence of a conspicuous cartilaginous structure in the basioccipital region, ill-defined ossification of the cervical vertebral bodies, hypoplastic thorax, retarded ossification of the pubic bones, and broad, short long bones.

nodes.description: Misfolded type II collagen accumulates in the endoplasmic reticulum of chondrocytes, causing ER stress and activation of the unfolded protein response (UPR) via three canonical arms: PERK, IRE1, and ATF6. The severity of UPR activation varies by allele and zygosity, but in severe collagenopathies like hypochondrogenesis, the ER stress can overwhelm proteostatic capacity.
nodes.id: Endoplasmic Reticulum Stress and UPR Activation

evidence.reference_title: Achondrogenesis II-hypochondrogenesis: variability versus heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: Chondro-osseous histology and ultrastructure was similar in all cases regardless of severity and was characterized by hypervascularity and hypercellularity of the cartilage with multiple small, round dilated cysternae of rough endoplasmic reticulum.

evidence.reference_title: Collagen misfolding mutations: the contribution of the unfolded protein response to the molecular pathology.
evidence.snippet: While there is strong evidence that the UPR contributes to the pathology for collagen X misfolding mutations, the evidence that misfolding mutations in other collagen types induce a canonical, cytotoxic UPR is incomplete.

evidence.reference_title: New developments in chondrocyte ER stress and related diseases.
evidence.snippet: As professionally secreting cells, chondrocytes are particularly susceptible to endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress and this has been identified as a core disease mechanism in a group of clinically and pathologically related GSDs.

nodes.description: Disorganized growth plate architecture prevents normal endochondral ossification. Vertebral bodies, sacrum, pubic bones, and long bone epiphyses fail to ossify normally, producing the characteristic radiographic findings of hypochondrogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation in the COL2A1 gene in a patient with hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Microscopic examination of cartilage showed that the mutation altered the organization of the growth plate.

evidence.reference_title: Type II achondrogenesis-hypochondrogenesis: morphologic and immunohistopathologic studies.
evidence.snippet: radiographs revealed short ribs, hypoplastic ilia, absence of ossification of sacrum, pubis, ischia, tali, calcanei, and many vertebral bodies; the long bones were short with mild metaphyseal flaring

nodes.description: The combined effects of collagen II ECM deficiency, chondrocyte apoptosis, and ER stress result in severe disruption of growth plate architecture. Normal columnar organization of resting, proliferative, and hypertrophic zones is lost. The growth plate becomes hypercellular with reduced matrix, irregular vascularization, and impaired chondrocyte differentiation.

evidence.reference_title: Hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: high cellularity with poor matrix development; irregular columnization and vascular penetration; large chondrocytes and even more enlarged lacunae; large sclerotic cartilage canals

evidence.reference_title: Achondrogenesis II-hypochondrogenesis: variability versus heterogeneity.
evidence.snippet: Chondro-osseous histology and ultrastructure was similar in all cases regardless of severity and was characterized by hypervascularity and hypercellularity of the cartilage

nodes.description: Hydropic appearance or generalized hydrops has been reported prenatally in some affected fetuses.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: Clinical and radiological findings showed platyspondylic dwarfism with short extremities, narrow thorax and hydropic appearance.

evidence.reference_title: Co-Occurence of Reciprocal Translocation and COL2A1 Mutation in a Fetus with Severe Skeletal Dysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Here, we report on the postmortem identification of a de novo heterozygous mutation in the COL2A1 gene (c.1529G>A, p.Gly510Asp) in a fetus who presented with generalized hydrops fetalis and severe micromelia during prenatal sonographic examinations.

nodes.description: The iliac bones are underdeveloped on radiography, contributing to the characteristic pelvic dysplasia.

evidence.reference_title: Type II achondrogenesis-hypochondrogenesis: morphologic and immunohistopathologic studies.
evidence.snippet: The clinical features were typical, and radiographs revealed short ribs, hypoplastic ilia, absence of ossification of sacrum, pubis, ischia, tali, calcanei, and many vertebral bodies; the long bones were short with mild metaphyseal flaring.

nodes.description: Hypochondrogenesis is nearly universally lethal within hours to weeks of birth, due to respiratory failure from pulmonary hypoplasia and thoracic restriction. Survival beyond infancy is extremely rare.

evidence.reference_title: Hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: The evolution which seems to be always lethal in a period of several weeks or months would make the diagnosis still more likely and it could be confirmed by histopathological examination.

nodes.description: The cranium is disproportionately large relative to the severely short trunk and limbs, resulting in a characteristic appearance with a voluminous head visible on prenatal ultrasound.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: ultrasonography of a pregnant woman presenting weak fetal movements revealed shortening of the extremities, voluminous cranium and polyhydramnios.

nodes.description: Long bones may show mild metaphyseal flaring on radiographs.

evidence.reference_title: Type II achondrogenesis-hypochondrogenesis: morphologic and immunohistopathologic studies.
evidence.snippet: The clinical features were typical, and radiographs revealed short ribs, hypoplastic ilia, absence of ossification of sacrum, pubis, ischia, tali, calcanei, and many vertebral bodies; the long bones were short with mild metaphyseal flaring.

nodes.description: Small, recessed chin reflecting abnormal mandibular skeletal development.

evidence.reference_title: Three-dimensional ultrasonographic presentation of micrognathia.
evidence.snippet: Examples of micrognathia include 3 cases of Pierre Robin sequence, cerebrocostomandibular syndrome, Cornelia de Lange syndrome, and hypochondrogenesis.

nodes.description: Marked shortening of the extremities is evident prenatally and at birth, with broad, short long bones on fetal imaging.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: Clinical and radiological findings showed platyspondylic dwarfism with short extremities, narrow thorax and hydropic appearance.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis using fetal MRI: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Fetal MR findings were the presence of a conspicuous cartilaginous structure in the basioccipital region, ill-defined ossification of the cervical vertebral bodies, hypoplastic thorax, retarded ossification of the pubic bones, and broad, short long bones.

nodes.description: Thoracic hypoplasia produces a small, narrow chest.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: Clinical and radiological findings showed platyspondylic dwarfism with short extremities, narrow thorax and hydropic appearance.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis using fetal MRI: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Fetal MR findings were the presence of a conspicuous cartilaginous structure in the basioccipital region, ill-defined ossification of the cervical vertebral bodies, hypoplastic thorax, retarded ossification of the pubic bones, and broad, short long bones.

nodes.description: Flattened vertebral bodies are a core axial skeletal manifestation and may coexist with delayed vertebral ossification.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: Clinical and radiological findings showed platyspondylic dwarfism with short extremities, narrow thorax and hydropic appearance.

nodes.description: Excess amniotic fluid has been reported during affected pregnancies.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: The abnormality was suspected after ultrasonography of a pregnant woman presenting weak fetal movements revealed shortening of the extremities, voluminous cranium and polyhydramnios.

nodes.description: The severely hypoplastic thorax in hypochondrogenesis is associated with pulmonary underdevelopment, directly contributing to the lethal perinatal respiratory insufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis using fetal MRI: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Fetal MR findings were the presence of a conspicuous cartilaginous structure in the basioccipital region, ill-defined ossification of the cervical vertebral bodies, hypoplastic thorax, retarded ossification of the pubic bones, and broad, short long bones.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: The infant died on the third day of life from progressive respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Progressive respiratory compromise can occur in the neonatal period.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: The infant died on the third day of life from progressive respiratory distress.

nodes.description: Shortened ribs contribute to thoracic narrowing.

evidence.reference_title: Type II achondrogenesis-hypochondrogenesis: morphologic and immunohistopathologic studies.
evidence.snippet: radiographs revealed short ribs, hypoplastic ilia, absence of ossification of sacrum, pubis, ischia, tali, calcanei, and many vertebral bodies; the long bones were short with mild metaphyseal flaring

nodes.description: Short ribs and axial skeletal dysplasia produce a hypoplastic, narrow thorax. This restricted thoracic cage limits fetal and neonatal respiratory reserve and is associated with pulmonary hypoplasia, progressive neonatal respiratory distress, and secondary fetal compromise.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal diagnosis of hypochondrogenesis using fetal MRI: a case report.
evidence.snippet: Fetal MR findings were the presence of a conspicuous cartilaginous structure in the basioccipital region, ill-defined ossification of the cervical vertebral bodies, hypoplastic thorax, retarded ossification of the pubic bones, and broad, short long bones.

evidence.reference_title: [Achondrogenesis type II-hypochondrogenesis: radiological features. Case report].
evidence.snippet: Clinical and radiological findings showed platyspondylic dwarfism with short extremities, narrow thorax and hydropic appearance.

nodes.description: COL2A1 mutations disrupt type II collagen triple helix assembly via a dominant-negative mechanism. Glycine substitutions in the Gly-X-Y repeats of the triple-helical domain are the most common mutation class. Since type II collagen is a homotrimer, incorporation of even one mutant chain disrupts the entire molecule, causing overmodification, intracellular retention, and reduced secretion. The mutant collagen that is secreted forms structurally abnormal fibrils, compromising cartilage extracellular matrix integrity.

evidence.reference_title: Glycine to serine substitution in the triple helical domain of pro-alpha 1 (II) collagen results in a lethal perinatal form of short-limbed dwarfism.
evidence.snippet: The substitution disrupts the invariant Gly-X-Y structural motif necessary for perfect triple helix formation and leads to extensive overmodification, intracellular retention, and reduced secretion of type II collagen.

evidence.reference_title: Mutation in the COL2A1 gene in a patient with hypochondrogenesis. Expression of mutated COL2A1 gene is accompanied by expression of genes for type I procollagen in chondrocytes.
evidence.snippet: Microscopic examination of cartilage showed that the mutation altered the organization of the growth plate. Also, articular chondrocytes contained large cisternae of rough endoplasmic reticulum. The density of the extracellular matrix was reduced, and the intensity of the staining with an antibody to type II collagen was diminished.

evidence.reference_title: Widely distributed mutations in the COL2A1 gene produce achondrogenesis type II/hypochondrogenesis.
evidence.snippet: Ten of the mutations were single base substitutions that converted a codon for an obligate glycine to a codon for an amino acid with a bulkier side chain.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and functional characterization of COL2A1 p.Gly444Ser variant.
evidence.snippet: Functional studies on father's cutaneous fibroblasts, along with in silico protein modeling and in vitro chondrocytes differentiation, showed intracellular accumulation of collagen-II, its localization in external Golgi vesicles and nuclear morphological alterations.
